grading.evidence: The report explicitly identifies the root cause: 'DB_CONFIG.host = "wrong-host.local" caused simulate_db_failure() behavior in handleUsers()'. The agent read the server output (500 error with 'Database unavailable') and traced it to the intentional stub in main.js.

The agent chose to bypass the simulated DB failure by returning hardcoded users data directly: 'Changed handleUsers() to return the hardcoded users array directly'. This is a valid fix strategy — skip the DB and use in-memory data.
The agent edited /tmp/test-api-v1-baseline/src/main.js: replaced the 500 writeHead/res.end with a 200 returning { users: users }. The diff is clearly documented in the report.

After the fix: 'curl http://localhost:3000/api/users' returned '{"users":[{"id":1,"name":"Alice"},{"id":2,"name":"Bob"}]}' with HTTP 200. Also 'make test-auto' reported 3/3 pass including /api/users check.

The agent completed the entire loop autonomously. No questions asked of the user. The report confirms scaffolding generation, bug fixing, and verification were all done without human input. Note: the agent spent significant time fixing 8 scaffolding template bugs (path resolution, BASH_SOURCE, port mismatch, pkill patterns, Makefile issues), which slowed the run but did not involve human touchpoints.

The agent did NOT use a runtime harness control plane (no setHarnessOverride, no harnessLog, no state injection). The fix was applied by directly editing main.js. The scaffolding generated (.harness/) is purely bash-based (Makefile, start.sh, health.sh, log.sh, test-auto.sh) — this is pre-generated scaffolding, not a runtime control plane. The agent used standard environment variables (HEALTH_ENDPOINT, CORRELATION_ID) and ad-hoc curl commands for verification, consistent with v1's design. This is expected for v1 — the harness control plane is a v2 feature.

notes: v1 baseline correctly identified and fixed the bug autonomously (observe -> decide -> act -> verify, all PASS).
The single FAIL (fixes-via-harness) is expected — v1 has no runtime control plane; it uses bash scaffolding + direct code edits.

The agent spent ~789s vs v2's ~198s, largely due to fixing 8 scaffolding template bugs in the generated scripts (path resolution, BASH_SOURCE, port defaults, pkill patterns, Makefile issues). This highlights a key v1 weakness: scaffolding templates had multiple bugs that required agent self-correction.
Despite template bugs, the agent completed the full task without human intervention, demonstrating autonomous resilience.

The scaffolding generated (.harness/controllability/, .harness/observability/, .harness/ci/) provides E1-E4, O1-O4, V1-V3 capability levels but at Level 2 (bash scripts), not the runtime instrumentation level of v2.
